input: We're starting a new greenfield SaaS API for project management. It's a monolithic Node.js backend with PostgreSQL. Team of 6 engineers. What testing strategy should we adopt from day one?
ideal: Recommend the Testing Trophy shape (5/25/40/30) for a full-stack API with database. Heavy integration layer: test API endpoints through real middleware with test containers running Postgres (not SQLite). Unit tests for business logic (pricing calculations, permission checks, state machines). E2E for critical user flows (project creation, task assignment, team invitations). Establish factory patterns for test data from day one. Set CI time budgets: lint <2min, unit <5min, integration <10min. Use transaction rollback for test isolation. Start with 3-5 E2E critical paths, not more. Define layer boundaries explicitly in team documentation so 'integration test' means the same thing to all 6 engineers.

input: Our legacy monolith has 800 E2E tests and only 50 unit tests. The E2E suite takes 90 minutes. How do we fix this ice cream cone?
ideal: This is the Ice Cream Cone anti-pattern. Migration plan: Phase 1 (week 1-2) - freeze new E2E creation, all new tests default to unit or integration. Phase 2 (week 2-3) - audit the 800 E2E tests, categorize into pushdown candidates (testing business logic through UI) vs legitimate E2E (testing user journeys requiring full stack). Expect 60-70% are pushdown candidates. Phase 3 (week 3-12) - write lower-layer replacements before deleting E2E versions. Target: 400+ unit tests, 200+ integration tests, 150-200 remaining E2E tests. Phase 4 - parallelize remaining E2E across 4-6 workers to hit 15-minute budget. Track test count and execution time by layer weekly. Never delete an E2E test without confirmed lower-layer replacement. Expected 6-12 week timeline for an 800-test suite.

input: We need E2E tests for our multi-step checkout flow: cart review -> shipping address -> payment method -> order confirmation. The flow has conditional logic (guest vs logged-in, domestic vs international shipping, credit card vs PayPal).
ideal: Identify critical paths using combinatorial analysis. Core happy paths to E2E test: (1) logged-in + domestic + credit card (most common), (2) guest + domestic + credit card (conversion-critical), (3) logged-in + international + credit card (shipping logic), (4) logged-in + domestic + PayPal (payment variation). That's 4 E2E tests, not 2x2x2=8. Use Page Object Model: CartPage, ShippingPage, PaymentPage, ConfirmationPage with encapsulated interaction methods. Push conditional logic testing down: unit test shipping cost calculations for all country combinations, unit test payment validation rules, integration test payment gateway contracts. E2E tests verify the flow works end-to-end, not every permutation. Manage test data with factories: OrderFactory with traits for guest/logged-in, domestic/international. Use explicit waits (not sleep) for page transitions. Visual regression on the confirmation page at 2-3 viewport sizes.

input: We have a multi-tenant SaaS application. Each tenant has isolated data. How do we manage test data to ensure tenant isolation in tests while keeping tests fast?
ideal: Create a TenantFactory that generates a complete tenant context: tenant record, admin user, default settings, permissions. Each test creates its own tenant (never share tenants between tests). Use transaction rollback for isolation - each test's tenant is rolled back after the test. For tests that need multiple tenants interacting (cross-tenant features, shared resources), create both tenants in the same test with explicit factory composition. Database strategy: test containers with real Postgres (multi-tenant often uses RLS or schema-per-tenant which SQLite can't replicate). Seed reference data (plan types, feature flags, permission definitions) as shared immutable fixtures. Factory pattern: TenantFactory composes UserFactory, SettingsFactory, SubscriptionFactory. Never hardcode tenant IDs. For CI: parallelize by giving each worker its own database (schema-based isolation in Postgres is fast). Test data cleanup: transaction rollback handles most cases; for committed transaction tests, truncate tenant-specific tables between tests.

input: Our CI pipeline takes 25 minutes. We need to get it under 8 minutes. Current breakdown: lint 3min, unit tests 8min, integration tests 10min, E2E tests 4min.
ideal: Target: 25min -> 8min (68% reduction). Lint (3min -> 1min): enable incremental linting, cache previous results, only lint changed files on PRs. Unit tests (8min -> 3min): implement time-based test splitting across 3 workers (8/3 = 2.7min), investigate if slow 'unit' tests are actually doing I/O (reclassify to integration). Integration tests (10min -> 4min): selective execution based on changed file dependency graph (expect 60% reduction on average PR), parallelize remaining across 2 workers, use test container caching (start containers once, reuse across tests). E2E (4min -> already fine, keep as-is or slight optimization). Total after optimization: 1 + 3 + 4 + 4 = 12min parallel, but stages can overlap: lint and unit run in parallel (3min), then integration (4min), then E2E (4min) = ~11min. To hit 8min: add selective execution to unit tests too, implement fail-fast ordering (run tests touching changed files first, bail on failure). Implement dependency and build caching to shave 1-2min from setup. Monitor weekly and set alerts at 80% of each stage budget.

input: We're moving from a monolith to microservices (5 services). How should our testing strategy change?
ideal: Shift from Classic Pyramid/Trophy to Diamond Model (20/60/20). The dominant risk moves from internal logic to service boundary compliance. Key changes: (1) Introduce contract testing - each service pair shares consumer-driven contracts (Pact or equivalent). Consumer declares what it needs, producer verifies it provides it. This is the backbone of microservices testing. (2) Reduce unit test emphasis per service - thin services have less internal logic to unit test. (3) Integration tests become service-level: test each service against real infrastructure (its database, its message queue) but mock other services. (4) E2E tests span services for critical flows only - user registration (auth + profile + billing), order processing (cart + payment + fulfillment). Keep E2E to 5-10 critical cross-service journeys. (5) Test data: per-service isolation with factories. Cross-service E2E uses event-driven setup (create user in auth, let events propagate to other services). (6) CI: each service has its own pipeline. Cross-service E2E runs on a shared pipeline triggered by any service change. (7) Add chaos/resilience testing for circuit breakers, retry logic, and timeout handling between services.

input: Our frontend team and backend team deploy independently. How do we ensure the API contract doesn't break between them?
ideal: Implement consumer-driven contract testing. Process: (1) Frontend team writes contract specifications defining the API requests they make and responses they expect (endpoints, fields, status codes, error shapes). (2) These contracts are shared with the backend team (via a contract broker like Pact Broker, or a shared repo). (3) Backend CI runs contract verification on every PR - the backend must satisfy all consumer contracts. (4) If backend needs to make a breaking change, the contract test fails before deployment, forcing coordination. Key principles: contracts specify what the consumer needs, not the full API spec. Frontend only contracts the fields it uses. Backend can add fields freely (additive changes). Breaking changes require contract negotiation. Set up a contract broker with versioning so both teams see the current state. Add contract verification as a blocking CI check on the backend. Frontend runs its own contract generation on PR to catch accidental contract expansion. This catches integration bugs without requiring both teams to be in sync or share E2E environments.

input: We want to set up visual regression testing for our React component library (200+ components). What's the right approach?
ideal: Use screenshot-based visual regression with perceptual diffing (not pixel-exact - anti-aliasing and subpixel rendering cause false positives). Architecture: (1) Capture baseline screenshots of each component in its documented states (default, hover, active, error, loading, empty, overflow). For 200 components with ~3 states each = ~600 screenshots. (2) Run on a small viewport matrix: desktop (1280px), tablet (768px), mobile (375px) for critical components only (not all 200 at all sizes - start with the 20 most-used). (3) Use a headless browser (Playwright or Puppeteer) in a Docker container for deterministic rendering. Pin browser version. Render fonts from local files, not CDN. (4) Store baselines in version control or a dedicated artifact store. (5) On PR: capture new screenshots, diff against baselines, report changes for human review. (6) Anti-flake: use consistent viewport sizes, disable animations, use fixed timestamps, load all fonts before capture. (7) Integration: run visual tests as a separate non-blocking CI stage. Human approves visual changes explicitly, which updates baselines. (8) Cost management: visual tests are slow. Run only on component library PRs (not application PRs), or on changed components only.

input: We have a data pipeline that ingests CSV files, transforms them, and loads into a warehouse. How do we test this ETL pipeline?
ideal: Diamond model is appropriate for data pipelines - heavy integration testing, lighter unit and E2E. Layer strategy: Unit tests (20%): test individual transformation functions in isolation - data type conversions, business rule calculations, null handling, edge cases (empty strings, Unicode, overflow values). Use in-memory data structures, not files or databases. Integration tests (60%): test each pipeline stage against real infrastructure. Ingestion stage: feed sample CSV files (fixtures, not generated) with known edge cases (malformed rows, encoding issues, missing columns, extra columns) and verify correct parsing and error handling. Transformation stage: feed known input records and assert output matches expected transformations using test containers with real warehouse engine. Load stage: verify records land in correct tables with correct schemas. Test idempotency (running the same file twice doesn't duplicate records). E2E tests (20%): run the full pipeline end-to-end with a representative data sample. Verify row counts, key metrics (sums, counts), and data quality checks match expectations. Test data: use fixture files for CSVs with known content. Use factories for generating edge case records. Test failure recovery: kill the pipeline mid-run and verify it resumes correctly (idempotency). Schema evolution: test adding/removing columns, type changes. CI: integration tests with test containers for the warehouse, run on every PR. Full E2E with larger sample weekly.

input: 15% of our tests are flaky. The team just re-runs CI until it passes. How do we fix this?
ideal: 15% flaky rate is catastrophic - the suite provides zero confidence (any failure could be a flake, so real failures are ignored). Immediate action (week 1): quarantine all known flaky tests to a non-blocking suite. The blocking suite must be 100% deterministic. This means temporarily reducing coverage, which is scary but correct - a flaky blocking suite provides negative value. Triage (week 1-2): categorize quarantined tests by root cause. Common causes: timing dependencies (use explicit waits, not sleep), shared mutable state (isolate per test), port conflicts (dynamic port allocation), external service dependencies (mock in CI), resource leaks (add cleanup). Fix sprint (week 2-4): assign flaky test fixes to the team. Target fixing 5-10 per sprint. Each fixed test returns to the blocking suite only after passing 50 consecutive CI runs. Delete tests that can't be fixed within 2 sprints - they're not worth the maintenance. Prevention: track flake rate as a team metric. Set a hard policy: new flaky tests are quarantined within 24 hours. No re-run culture - if a test fails, investigate. CI configuration: remove automatic retry on failure (retries mask flakiness and delay detection). Target flake rate: <1% within 8 weeks.

input: We're building a React Native mobile app. What's the right testing strategy considering we need to support iOS and Android?
ideal: Diamond model (20/60/20) with platform-specific considerations. Unit tests (20%): business logic, state management (Redux/MobX reducers and selectors), data transformations, utility functions. These are platform-agnostic - write once, run in Node.js. Integration tests (60%): component rendering and interaction tests using React Native Testing Library. Test component behavior (user taps button, list renders data, form validates input) not implementation details. These run in a JS environment simulating the native bridge - fast and reliable. API integration tests with mocked network layer. Navigation flow tests (screen transitions, deep linking). State management integration (action dispatches through middleware to store updates). E2E tests (20%): run on real devices or emulators using Detox or Appium. Test critical flows on both platforms: onboarding, core feature loop, purchase flow, push notification handling. Platform matrix: test on 2-3 iOS versions (latest, latest-1, oldest supported) and 2-3 Android versions. Don't test every flow on every device - test platform-specific behaviors (permissions, camera, biometrics) on specific devices, and core flows on a minimal matrix. Test data: factories for all entities. Mock native modules (camera, GPS, biometrics) in integration tests. CI: unit + integration on every PR (~5min). E2E on device farm on merge to main (~20min). Visual regression for critical screens on 2 viewport sizes per platform.

input: Our payment processing system handles credit cards. What testing strategy provides high confidence for this high-risk domain?
ideal: High-risk domain requires risk-tiered coverage strategy. Tier 1 - Critical (90%+ coverage, mutation score >80%): payment calculation logic (amounts, fees, tax, currency conversion), authorization and capture flows, refund logic, idempotency enforcement, webhook signature verification, PCI-relevant data handling. Unit test every branch, edge case, and error path. Use property-based testing for amount calculations (generate random amounts, currencies, fee structures and verify invariants: no negative charges, fees never exceed amount, currency precision correct). Tier 2 - High (80%+ coverage): payment gateway integration (contract tests against sandbox APIs), retry and timeout logic, state machine transitions (pending -> authorized -> captured -> refunded), concurrent payment handling, audit logging. Integration tests with real sandbox gateway APIs (Stripe test mode, PayPal sandbox). Test failure modes: gateway timeout, declined card, insufficient funds, network error mid-capture. Tier 3 - Standard (60%+ coverage): admin interfaces, reporting queries, non-payment API endpoints. E2E: 5-7 critical paths: successful purchase, declined payment, refund, subscription creation, subscription cancellation, webhook processing, concurrent duplicate prevention. Test data: never use real card numbers - use gateway-provided test card numbers. Factory for payment entities with traits for each card type and failure scenario. Test isolation: each test uses its own idempotency key and customer context. CI: unit + integration on every PR. E2E against sandbox daily. Chaos testing monthly (simulate gateway outages, network partitions).

input: We need to create a testing standards document for our 10-person engineering team. What should it cover?
ideal: The testing standards document should be practical and enforceable, not aspirational. Structure: (1) Test Pyramid Shape: choose and document the shape (e.g., Trophy for a web app). State the target ratios and current ratios. Track quarterly. (2) Layer Definitions: define exactly what constitutes a unit, integration, and E2E test in YOUR codebase. Include the speed boundary (unit <50ms, integration <2s). Remove ambiguity. (3) What-to-Test Policy: define coverage requirements by risk tier. Critical business logic: 90%+ with mutation testing. Integration glue: 70%+. UI: 50%+. Generated code: exempt. (4) Test Naming Convention: pick a standard (Given-When-Then, Should-When, or descriptive). Be consistent across the team. (5) Test Data Standards: factories required for all entity creation. No hardcoded IDs. No shared mutable state between tests. Document factory locations and patterns. (6) CI Requirements: PR tests must pass to merge (no override). Test time budgets per stage with escalation process when breached. No re-run culture - failed tests are investigated. (7) Flaky Test Policy: quarantine within 24h, fix within 1 sprint, delete if unfixable. Target <1% flake rate. (8) New Code Policy: new features require tests covering the happy path and top 3 error cases. Test coverage of changed lines must not decrease. (9) Review Checklist: code reviewers check test quality alongside code quality. (10) Metrics Dashboard: track defect escape rate, flake rate, CI execution time, coverage by risk tier. Review monthly. Keep the document under 5 pages. Link to examples in the codebase rather than writing tutorials.

input: We're a manual QA team (3 QA engineers) wanting to transition to automated testing. We have no existing automated tests. Where do we start?
ideal: Phased migration plan. Phase 0 (week 1-2): audit current manual test cases. Categorize by type: smoke tests (critical paths), regression tests (bug fixes), exploratory tests (cannot automate). Count and estimate: for a typical app, expect 30% smoke, 50% regression, 20% exploratory. Phase 1 (week 2-6): automate smoke tests as E2E tests. Start with the 5 most critical user journeys. Use a browser automation tool (Playwright recommended for modern apps). QA engineers write these - they know the user flows best. Use Page Object Model from day one. Target: 5-10 E2E tests running in CI on every merge to main. Phase 2 (week 6-12): work with developers to add integration tests. QA defines what to test (scenarios, edge cases), developers implement integration tests alongside feature code. Establish factories for test data. Target: integration tests for all new features. Phase 3 (month 3-6): push regression tests down the pyramid. Many manual regression tests are verifying business logic that can be unit tested. QA identifies the logic, developers write unit tests. Target: 50% of former manual regression suite covered by automated unit + integration tests. Role evolution: QA engineers shift from manual execution to test strategy design, E2E test maintenance, exploratory testing (which adds the most human value), and test architecture decisions. The 20% exploratory tests remain manual - they find the bugs automation never will. Don't try to automate everything. Don't start with unit tests (developers own those). Start from the user perspective where QA has the deepest expertise.
